infobox.value: ([/duːrænt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is a city in and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Bryan County, Oklahoma](/wiki/Bryan_County,_Oklahoma "Bryan County, Oklahoma"), United States. The population was 18,589 in the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"). It is the home of the headquarters of the [Choctaw Nation](/wiki/Choctaw_Nation "Choctaw Nation"), and is the largest settlement on the reservation, ranking ahead of [McAlester](/wiki/McAlester,_Oklahoma "McAlester, Oklahoma") and [Poteau](/wiki/Poteau,_Oklahoma "Poteau, Oklahoma"). Durant is the principal city of the Durant [Micropolitan Statistical Area](/wiki/List_of_United_States_micropolitan_areas "List of United States micropolitan areas"), which had a population of 46,067 in 2020.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Durant is also part of the [Dallas–Fort Worth Combined Statistical Area](/wiki/Dallas%E2%80%93Fort_Worth_metroplex "Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex"), anchoring the northern edge.[[5]](#cite_note-violentcrime-5)

**Durant** ([/duːrænt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is a city in and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Bryan County, Oklahoma](/wiki/Bryan_County,_Oklahoma "Bryan County, Oklahoma"), United States. The population was 18,589 in the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"). It is the home of the headquarters of the [Choctaw Nation](/wiki/Choctaw_Nation "Choctaw Nation"), and is the largest settlement on the reservation, ranking ahead of [McAlester](/wiki/McAlester,_Oklahoma "McAlester, Oklahoma") and [Poteau](/wiki/Poteau,_Oklahoma "Poteau, Oklahoma"). Durant is the principal city of the Durant [Micropolitan Statistical Area](/wiki/List_of_United_States_micropolitan_areas "List of United States micropolitan areas"), which had a population of 46,067 in 2020.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Durant is also part of the [Dallas–Fort Worth Combined Statistical Area](/wiki/Dallas%E2%80%93Fort_Worth_metroplex "Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex"), anchoring the northern edge.[[5]](#cite_note-violentcrime-5)

The city was founded by Dixon Durant, a [Choctaw](/wiki/Choctaw "Choctaw") who lived in the area,[[6]](#cite_note-phipps-6) after the [MK&T railroad](/wiki/Missouri%E2%80%93Kansas%E2%80%93Texas_Railroad "Missouri–Kansas–Texas Railroad") came through the [Indian Territory](/wiki/Indian_Territory "Indian Territory") in the early 1870s. It became the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of Bryan County[[7]](#cite_note-GR6-7) in 1907 after Oklahoma statehood.

Durant is home to [Southeastern Oklahoma State University](/wiki/Southeastern_Oklahoma_State_University "Southeastern Oklahoma State University") and the headquarters of the Choctaw Nation. The city is officially known as the [Magnolia](/wiki/Magnolia "Magnolia") Capital of Oklahoma.[[8]](#cite_note-sosu_master_plan-8) The city and its micropolitan are a major part of the [Texoma](/wiki/Texoma "Texoma") region.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Durant_June_2018_02_%28Bryan_County_Courthouse%29.jpg/250px-Durant_June_2018_02_%28Bryan_County_Courthouse%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durant_June_2018_02_(Bryan_County_Courthouse).jpg)

Bryan County Courthouse and Confederate monument

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Durant_city_hall.JPG/250px-Durant_city_hall.JPG)](/wiki/File:Durant_city_hall.JPG)

Durant City Hall

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Oklahoma_-_Durant_-_NARA_-_68147128_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Oklahoma_-_Durant_-_NARA_-_68147128_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oklahoma_-_Durant_-_NARA_-_68147128_(cropped).jpg)

Durant in 1936

The Durant area was once claimed by both Spain and France before officially becoming part of the United States after the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase") and [Adams–Onís Treaty](/wiki/Adams%E2%80%93On%C3%ADs_Treaty "Adams–Onís Treaty"). During the 1820s and 1830s the area was designated as part of the Choctaw Nation in the southern Indian Territory. During the [Indian removals](/wiki/Indian_removal "Indian removal") the Choctaws followed the [Choctaw Trail of Tears](/wiki/Choctaw_Trail_of_Tears "Choctaw Trail of Tears") from their ancestral homeland in [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi "Mississippi") and [Alabama](/wiki/Alabama "Alabama") into this area. The Choctaw Nation originally extended from the [Mexican](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") border in the west (now part of the [Texas panhandle](/wiki/Texas_panhandle "Texas panhandle")) to the [Arkansas Territory](/wiki/Arkansas_Territory "Arkansas Territory") in the east, from the [Red River](/wiki/Red_River_of_the_South "Red River of the South") in the south to the [South Canadian River](/wiki/South_Canadian_River "South Canadian River") in the north. In 1855, the Choctaw and Chickasaws formally divided their land into two separate nations, with Durant remaining in Choctaw territory on the east. Chickasaw land extended west to the boundary that would divide Indian Territory from Oklahoma Territory after passage of the Oklahoma Organic Act in 1890.[[9]](#cite_note-milligan-9)

Pierre Durant and his four sons, all of French-Choctaw origin, made the journey up the Trail of Tears on the way to the southeastern part of the [Choctaw Nation](/wiki/Choctaw_Nation_of_Oklahoma "Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma") in 1832. The brothers, grown, with families of their own, established homesteads from the Arkansas line to Durant. One son, Fisher, married to a full-blood [Choctaw](/wiki/Choctaw "Choctaw"), found a beautiful location for a home between Durant's present Eighth and Ninth avenues. At the time of Durant's founding it was located in [Blue County](/wiki/Blue_County,_Choctaw_Nation "Blue County, Choctaw Nation"), a part of the [Pushmataha District](/wiki/Pushmataha_District "Pushmataha District") of the Choctaw Nation.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Fisher Durant's son Dixon Durant is recognized as the founder of Durant and is honored as its namesake. A minister, businessman and civic leader, Dixon Durant is credited with pastorates in local [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian"), [Congregational](/wiki/Congregational "Congregational") and [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist") churches. He established the first store selling general merchandise in 1873,[[9]](#cite_note-milligan-9) around the time of the 1872 creation of the [Missouri–Kansas–Texas Railroad](/wiki/Missouri%E2%80%93Kansas%E2%80%93Texas_Railroad "Missouri–Kansas–Texas Railroad") (Katy Railroad) siding at Durant, which was the initial impetus for establishing the community.[[9]](#cite_note-milligan-9)

The Missouri-Kansas and Texas Railway (also known as the MKT or "Katy") had already laid a line through the area that would become Durant by November 1882. A wheelless boxcar was parked on the siding there and named "Durant Station". Dixon Durant erected the first building, adjacent to the boxcar, where he opened a general store in 1873. The first post office, also named as Durant Station, Indian Territory, opened February 20, 1879, but closed on July 11, 1881.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirk198774-11) A.E. Fulsom was postmaster.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The [U.S. Postal Service](/wiki/U.S._Postal_Service "U.S. Postal Service") re-established the post office at the site as Durant on March 8, 1882, dropping the word "station" from the name.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirk198774-11) Beginning in 1882, the area was simply called Durant.[[9]](#cite_note-milligan-9)

W.H. Hilton was elected the first mayor of Durant.

A memorable event in Durant's rail history occurred on April 5, 1905. A special southbound Katy train stopped in the city with President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") aboard.[[13]](#cite_note-roosevelt-13)

In 1895, a fire destroyed the original business district, which had spread along the Katy tracks.

Calvin Institute was opened in 1894, representing the first institute of higher education in the immediate area, which was an outgrowth of Presbyterian mission work among the [Choctaw Indian nation](/wiki/Choctaw_Nation_of_Oklahoma "Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma"). Its success led it being reopened as a larger school, Durant Presbyterian College in 1901, later renamed as [Oklahoma Presbyterian College](/wiki/Oklahoma_Presbyterian_College "Oklahoma Presbyterian College").[[14]](#cite_note-Lost_Colleges-14) After statehood became effective on November 16, 1907, the state legislature created the Southeastern State Normal School at Durant, which opened March 6, 1909. This school was renamed Southeastern State Teachers College in 1921, and renamed again in 1974 as the present Southeastern Oklahoma State University.[[9]](#cite_note-milligan-9)

Further growth of the town was inhibited by its proximity to the larger town of Caddo (also on the Katy line) and the fact that Dixon Durant did not want to sell more of the land he had inherited to non-Indians. In the 1902-1903 timeframe, the [St. Louis, San Francisco and New Orleans Railroad](/wiki/St._Louis,_San_Francisco_and_New_Orleans_Railroad "St. Louis, San Francisco and New Orleans Railroad"), an affiliate of the [St. Louis and San Francisco Railway](/wiki/St._Louis_and_San_Francisco_Railway "St. Louis and San Francisco Railway") (also known as "SL&SF" or "Frisco") had intended to build an east–west line through Caddo, where it would intersect the Katy. A rapid land price increase near Caddo instead caused the Frisco to bypass it in favor of Durant.[[15]](#cite_note-nrhpdoc-15)[[16]](#cite_note-Museum-16)

In 1904, Durant was named in a grand jury instruction as a [sundown town](/wiki/Sundown_town "Sundown town") where a notice had been posted warning African Americans not to stay after dark.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Bryan County was created from Choctaw lands in 1907, the same time as statehood, and was named after [William Jennings Bryan](/wiki/William_Jennings_Bryan "William Jennings Bryan"). Bryan was nominated three times for President of the United States and at the age of 36 lost to [William McKinley](/wiki/William_McKinley "William McKinley"). He lost to McKinley again in 1900, and to [William H. Taft](/wiki/William_H._Taft "William H. Taft") in 1908. [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") appointed the county's namesake as [United States Secretary of State](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_State "United States Secretary of State") in 1913.

The Oklahoma Constitutional Convention selected Durant as the county seat for Bryan County, Oklahoma, which would supersede Blue County at statehood. In 1908, a special election ratified this choice over three other candidates for the honor: [Bokchito](/wiki/Bokchito,_Oklahoma "Bokchito, Oklahoma"), [Blue](/wiki/Blue,_Oklahoma "Blue, Oklahoma"), and Sterrett (later renamed [Calera](/wiki/Calera,_Oklahoma "Calera, Oklahoma")).[[15]](#cite_note-nrhpdoc-15)

The [Bryan County Courthouse](/wiki/Bryan_County_Courthouse_(Oklahoma) "Bryan County Courthouse (Oklahoma)") in downtown Durant was completed in 1917.[[15]](#cite_note-nrhpdoc-15) Before the courthouse stands a [Confederate monument](/wiki/Confederate_monument "Confederate monument"), erected in the same year by the [United Daughters of the Confederacy](/wiki/United_Daughters_of_the_Confederacy "United Daughters of the Confederacy").[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Eleven people were killed in Durant by a [tornado](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado") in [April 1919](/wiki/Tornado_outbreak_of_April_1919 "Tornado outbreak of April 1919").[[20]](#cite_note-NYT1919-20)

The town's population grew from 2,969 in 1900 to 5,330 in 1910, 12,823 in 1990, and to 13,549 in 2000.[[9]](#cite_note-milligan-9)

The [Durant Downtown Historic District](/wiki/Durant_Downtown_Historic_District "Durant Downtown Historic District") is listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").

### 1911 lynching

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1911 lynching")]

Main article: [Lynching of John Lee](/wiki/Lynching_of_John_Lee "Lynching of John Lee")

John Lee was an African American man who was [lynched](/wiki/Lynching "Lynching") on August 12, 1911, in Durant, Oklahoma. He was subjected to a brutal act of mob violence, denial of judicial due process, and the desecration of his body posthumously. The event is a reflection of the racial tensions and injustices prevalent in the United States during the early 20th century.

The book *[Without Sanctuary: Lynching Photography in America](https://withoutsanctuary.org/pics_16.html)* by Twin Palms Publishers includes a photo from Durant on August 13, 1911, of a group of townspeople burning the body of an African American man named John Lee.

The author of *Without Sanctuary* summarizes that, "According to the August 14 issue of the Durant Daily Democrat, Lee died at 11:15 p.m. at the hands of a posse of 500. An additional 1,500 citizens were estimated to have been involved in the manhunt. Reports indicate that Lee exchanged gunfire with his would-be captors until his ammunition was spent. The posse "calmly emptied their guns into his body". Lee was accused of a range of crimes, the most serious of which was the critical wounding of a white woman, Mrs. Fanny Campbell. She identified the corpse as that of her assailant. Mrs. Campbell expired later the same day. The mob took John Lee's body to a vacant lot near the railroad tracks, where they built a pyre of gathered lumber and set the remains on fire. It burned from nine in the morning until late in the afternoon. All that remained were ashes and a "few charred parcels". Whites rioted throughout the town of Durant. The city's remaining blacks were warned "not to let the sun go down on them here". All left by sunset. Rumors spread that blacks were organizing to return and avenge Lee's death. Durant's white citizens armed for the coming "race war". In several days, without additional violence, tensions calmed.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Durant_Oklahoma_Aerial_%2850996156576%29.jpg/250px-Durant_Oklahoma_Aerial_%2850996156576%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durant_Oklahoma_Aerial_(50996156576).jpg)

Aerial view of Durant

Durant is located in southeast Oklahoma, in a region named [Texoma](/wiki/Texoma "Texoma"), or Texomaland, because of its short distance from [Lake Texoma](/wiki/Lake_Texoma "Lake Texoma"). The city is also part of [Choctaw Country](/wiki/Kiamichi_Country "Kiamichi Country"), formerly [Kiamichi Country](/wiki/Kiamichi_Country "Kiamichi Country"). Its geographic coordinates are [33°59′59″N 96°23′5″W﻿ / ﻿33.99972°N 96.38472°W﻿ / 33.99972; -96.38472](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Durant,_Oklahoma&params=33_59_59_N_96_23_5_W_type:city) (33.999834, −96.384825).[[23]](#cite_note-GR1-23) It is approximately 14 miles (23 km) north of the Texas border at the [Red River](/wiki/Red_River_of_the_South "Red River of the South"). [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas") is about 88 miles (142 km) south of Durant. The north edge of the [Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex](/wiki/Dallas%E2%80%93Fort_Worth_metroplex "Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex"), in [McKinney](/wiki/McKinney,_Texas "McKinney, Texas"), is about 58 miles (93 km) to the south.

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city of Durant has a total area of 26.8 square miles (69.3 km2), of which 26.7 square miles (69.2 km2) is land and 0.077 square miles (0.2 km2), or 0.24%, is water.[[24]](#cite_note-Census_2010-24)

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Durant had a population of 18,589. The median age was 32.1 years. 24.2% of residents were under the age of 18 and 14.6% of residents were 65 years of age or older. For every 100 females there were 93.0 males, and for every 100 females age 18 and over there were 91.0 males age 18 and over.[[32]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-32)

88.5% of residents lived in urban areas, while 11.5% lived in rural areas.[[33]](#cite_note-Census2020DHC-33)

There were 7,189 households in Durant, of which 32.7% had children under the age of 18 living in them. Of all households, 36.5% were married-couple households, 20.4% were households with a male householder and no spouse or partner present, and 33.8% were households with a female householder and no spouse or partner present. About 30.5% of all households were made up of individuals and 11.8% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older.[[32]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-32)

There were 7,887 housing units, of which 8.9% were vacant. Among occupied housing units, 43.0% were owner-occupied and 57.0% were renter-occupied. The homeowner vacancy rate was 1.5% and the rental vacancy rate was 7.0%.[[32]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-32)

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

As of the census of 2010,[[35]](#cite_note-35) there were 15,856 people and 3,651 families residing in the city. The population density was 830 inhabitants per square mile (320/km2). There were 7,202 housing units. Nearly three fourths of the city's population (74.7%) self-identified as [white](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 13.3% self-identified as [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), and 2.2% self-identified as black or [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"). Less than a tenth of the population (7.1%) self-identified as [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)"), regardless of race. Individuals of mixed Native American and white heritage accounted for 4.8% of the population. Less than 1% of the population was [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)") or [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._Census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. Census)").

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

Durant's first census was recorded in 1900, and the population was 2,969.[[a]](#cite_note-36) The 2000 census reported Durant's population as 13,549.

There were 6,331 households in the city, of which 26% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 37.3% were married couples living together, 14.2% had a female householder with no husband present, and 42.3% were non-families. Individuals living alone accounted for 32.9% of households and 25.3% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.34 and the average family size was 2.96.

In the city, the population was spread out, with 22% under the age of 18, 18.6% from 18 to 24, 24.8% from 25 to 44, 20.4% from 45 to 64, and 14.2% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 30.9 years. There were slightly more females (51.4% of the population) than males (48.6%).

The median income for a household in the city was $35,135, and the median income for a family was $41,014. Males working full-time had a median income of $34,040 versus $26,197 for female full-time workers. The per capita income for the city was $18,009. About 21% of families and 28% of the population were below the poverty line, including 35% of those under age 18 and 14% of those age 65 or over.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Economy")]

Durant was ranked as the fastest growing rural city in Oklahoma in 2004, having the fastest growth rate outside of the [Oklahoma City](/wiki/Oklahoma_City "Oklahoma City") and [Tulsa](/wiki/Tulsa,_Oklahoma "Tulsa, Oklahoma") metropolitan areas.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

Durant's daytime population increases to approximately 20,000 people.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The city has a pull factor of 1.8–2.1 times its population and was named an [All-America City](/wiki/All-America_City "All-America City") finalist for 2006.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Since 1999, the Durant Economic Development Department, the Durant Industrial Authority and the City of Durant have developed over $600,000,000 in new investments to the city.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Durant currently leads the state in economic development.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] One of the city's strongest industries is tourism; attractions include [Lake Texoma](/wiki/Lake_Texoma "Lake Texoma"), the [Choctaw Casino Resort](/wiki/Choctaw_Casino_Resort "Choctaw Casino Resort"), and [Fort Washita](/wiki/Fort_Washita "Fort Washita"). [Manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing") and [distribution](/wiki/Distribution_(business) "Distribution (business)") are growing industries in Durant with several factories being constructed and planned.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/New_Choctaw_Casino_Resort.jpg/250px-New_Choctaw_Casino_Resort.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_Choctaw_Casino_Resort.jpg)

North building of Choctaw Casino Resort

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Choctaw_Nation_Headquarters_%2808-20-2023%29.jpg/250px-Choctaw_Nation_Headquarters_%2808-20-2023%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Choctaw_Nation_Headquarters_(08-20-2023).jpg)

Choctaw Nation Headquarters

The largest employer in Durant is the Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma, including the Choctaw Nation Headquarters and the Choctaw Casino Resort, which has two Choctaw Travel Plazas, two Choctaw casinos, the Choctaw Inn, and more facilities that are located in the resort. Over 5,400 people work for the Choctaw Nation in Durant. Though the capital of the Choctaw Nation is recognized as being [Tuskahoma](/wiki/Tuskahoma,_Oklahoma "Tuskahoma, Oklahoma"), the administrative offices are located in Durant.

In 1894, [Calvin Institute](/wiki/Calvin_Institute "Calvin Institute"), a school for Native American youths, was established in Durant. By 1899, it had attracted an enrollment of 300. It was renamed Durant Presbyterian College in 1900 and Oklahoma Presbyterian College in 1910. The support for the school came from the Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma, the federal government, and several denominational missionary boards. Because of financial difficulties, the school was closed as a learning institution in the late 1960s. Chief Clark David Gardner established the Choctaw Nation administrative offices at the old Oklahoma Presbyterian College Building in 1975.

In 1976, in cooperation with the Durant Chamber of Commerce and the owners of the buildings, the [Red River Valley Historical Association](/w/index.php?title=Red_River_Valley_Historical_Association&action=edit&redlink=1 "Red River Valley Historical Association (page does not exist)"), title was transferred to the [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States"). Ceremonies were held August 17, 1976, commemorating the title transfer. Reacquisition of this building allowed centralization of government which permits more effective utilization of personnel in administering current programs and developing future programs. The buildings has been renovated, and administration of many Choctaw programs are headquartered there.

Another important part of Durant's economy is the city's historic central business district and the retail district. In the past few years, downtown Durant has seen growth, renewal projects such as streetscaping, and new businesses arriving. Durant has a [Main Street program](/wiki/List_of_Main_Street_Programs_in_the_United_States "List of Main Street Programs in the United States").[[37]](#cite_note-38) The retail district is west of downtown, at the intersection of [U.S. 69](/wiki/U.S._Route_69_(Oklahoma) "U.S. Route 69 (Oklahoma)")/[75](/wiki/U.S._Route_75_(Oklahoma) "U.S. Route 75 (Oklahoma)") and [U.S. 70](/wiki/U.S._Route_70_in_Oklahoma "U.S. Route 70 in Oklahoma"), and is Durant's fastest growing area.

Cardinal Glass Industries has a [float glass](/wiki/Float_glass "Float glass") manufacturing facility in western Durant. It became operational in July 2004. This plant is number 20 for Cardinal Glass Industries.

[Big Lots](/wiki/Big_Lots "Big Lots") has a 1,200,000 sq ft (110,000 m2). distribution center in Durant that began operating in January 2004.

Durant is home to the headquarters of [First United Bank](/wiki/First_United_Bank "First United Bank"), one of the largest privately owned banks in the United States,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and [First Texoma National Bank](/w/index.php?title=First_Texoma_National_Bank&action=edit&redlink=1 "First Texoma National Bank (page does not exist)"). Indian Nation Wholesale, also headquartered in Durant, was the 15th largest [wholesaler](/wiki/Wholesale "Wholesale") in the United States as of 2008.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/World%27s_Largest_Peanut_Monument.jpg/250px-World%27s_Largest_Peanut_Monument.jpg)](/wiki/File:World%27s_Largest_Peanut_Monument.jpg)

The large peanut in Durant

Durant is home to the annual Magnolia Festival and the [Three Valley Museum](/wiki/Three_Valley_Museum "Three Valley Museum"). The festival is put on by the Durant Chamber of Commerce and Durant Main Street Program the weekend following [Memorial Day](/wiki/Memorial_Day "Memorial Day"). It features music, shopping, shows, and two pageants – the Magnolia Pageant and the Choctaw Princess Pageant.[[39]](#cite_note-magnoliafest-40)

The Oklahoma Legislature proclaimed Durant as the "Magnolia Capital of the World" in 1993. Every year since, the town has celebrated a Magnolia Festival during the weekend following Memorial Day.[[9]](#cite_note-milligan-9)

Durant has a "World's Largest Peanut" monument, a title it shares with two other monuments in Texas and [Ashburn, Georgia](/wiki/Ashburn,_Georgia "Ashburn, Georgia"). This monument to the [peanut](/wiki/Peanut "Peanut") growers in Bryan County is located on the front lawn of Durant's city hall. Dedicated in 1973, it includes a [time capsule](/wiki/Time_capsule "Time capsule") that contains historic and legal documents, which was unearthed in 2023.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

The Three Valley Museum houses historical artifacts of Bryan County. Opened in 1976, it is named for a book about Durant, *Queen of the Three Valleys* by Henry McCreary, which references the city's location in the middle of the [Red](/wiki/Red_River_of_the_South "Red River of the South"), [Blue River](/wiki/Blue_River_(Oklahoma) "Blue River (Oklahoma)"), and [Washita River](/wiki/Washita_River "Washita River") valleys. The museum's exhibits include an early law office, doctor's office, child's room, parlor, and general store.

The Oklahoma Shakespearean Festival is housed on the campus of Southeastern Oklahoma State University. Oklahoma Shakespearean Festival is one of the top summer theatre festivals in America. It has made Durant and Southeastern Oklahoma State University a destination for tourists, professional and aspiring actors and theatre artisans.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Sports")]

Although no major sports team is located in Durant, many sporting opportunities are found in the city, including the Durant Multi-Sports Complex, golf, soccer pitches, and baseball fields.

The Durant Multi-Sports Complex is an athletic facility located 2 mi (3.2 km) southeast of Durant, on [Highway 78](/wiki/Oklahoma_State_Highway_78 "Oklahoma State Highway 78"). The sports complex, dedicated on November 17, 2006, sits on 80 acres (32 ha), with 100 acres (40 ha) undeveloped. It consists of [baseball](/wiki/Baseball_field "Baseball field") and [softball](/wiki/Softball "Softball") fields, [soccer pitches](/wiki/Soccer_field "Soccer field"), and a [football](/wiki/American_football "American football") [stadium](/wiki/Stadium "Stadium")/track field. A 17-acre (69,000 m2) lake is under construction, and a [walking](/wiki/Walking_trail "Walking trail") and biking trail is planned to be built around the lake. The cost of the complex was $7 million.

### College athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: College athletics")]

Southeastern Oklahoma State University competes in [NCAA Division II](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II "NCAA Division II") in the [Great American Conference](/wiki/Great_American_Conference "Great American Conference"). The [Savage Storm](/wiki/Southeastern_Oklahoma_State_Savage_Storm "Southeastern Oklahoma State Savage Storm") compete in the following sports: baseball, men's and women's basketball, football, men's and women's tennis, men's golf, rodeo, women's cross country, softball, women's track & field and women's volleyball. In 2000, the baseball team won the Division II college world series.

## Parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Parks and recreation")]

The City of Durant maintains and operates 11 parks totaling more than 251 acres (1.02 km2). They include:

* [Durant Multi-Sports Complex](/wiki/Durant_Multi-Sports_Complex "Durant Multi-Sports Complex")
* Dixon Durant Park – formerly Northside Park, or Rocket Park, renamed in honor of the founder of Durant.
* [Carl Albert](/wiki/Carl_Albert "Carl Albert") Memorial Park & [public pool](/wiki/Public_pool "Public pool")
* Billy Miller Park
* Schuler Park
* Lake Durant

Less than 15 miles (24 km) away, [Lake Texoma](/wiki/Lake_Texoma "Lake Texoma") has between 8–10 million visitors every year and is the 12th largest lake in the United States, and also one of the largest reservoirs in the country, contributing to Durant's [economic](/wiki/Economic_growth "Economic growth") and [population growth](/wiki/Population_growth "Population growth").

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Government")]

Durant is governed by a [council-manager](/wiki/Council%E2%80%93manager_government "Council–manager government") form of municipal government. The [city manager](/wiki/City_manager "City manager") is the administrative leader of the government and is appointed by the [city council](/wiki/City_council "City council"). The city's ceremonial head is the [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor"), who is a voting member of the council with limited administrative power.

The current interim city manager is Rick Rumsey, who replaced Lisa Taylor in a council vote.[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-44) The current mayor is Martin Tucker.[[44]](#cite_note-45) The city is divided into four wards with a member of the city council from each. There is also an at-large city council member. The current mayor also represents Ward 1.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Education")]

### Higher

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Higher")]

Main article: [Southeastern Oklahoma State University](/wiki/Southeastern_Oklahoma_State_University "Southeastern Oklahoma State University")

Durant is home to [Southeastern Oklahoma State University](/wiki/Southeastern_Oklahoma_State_University "Southeastern Oklahoma State University"), which has about 5,200 enrolled students. It is the only university in Oklahoma to offer a Master of Science in Aviation and Space. Southeastern Oklahoma State University offers degrees through its Aviation Sciences Institute, the largest aviation program in the state. The main campus in Durant has facilities at the airport to support flight training and facilities on campus to facilitate the management options in business, maintenance, safety, and security. The institute offers the undergraduate management degrees as well as the Master of Science degree in Aerospace Administration & Logistics.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

Southeastern Oklahoma State University also offers a doctoral degree, Ed.D., in Applied Educational Studies with the Aviation and Space Science specialization and a Graduate Certificate in Aviation Security.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

### Career and technical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Career and technical")]

Durant is home to the [Kiamichi Technology Center](/wiki/Kiamichi_Technology_Center "Kiamichi Technology Center"), which has eight other locations in [southeastern Oklahoma](/wiki/Southeastern_Oklahoma "Southeastern Oklahoma"). Kiamichi is part of the [Oklahoma Department of Career and Technology Education](/wiki/Oklahoma_Department_of_Career_and_Technology_Education "Oklahoma Department of Career and Technology Education").

### Elementary and secondary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Elementary and secondary")]

See also: [Durant Independent School District](/wiki/Durant_Independent_School_District "Durant Independent School District")

The [Durant Independent School District](/wiki/Durant_Independent_School_District "Durant Independent School District") is the largest school district in southeastern Oklahoma and serves approximately 3,100 students.[[48]](#cite_note-edcomdurant-49) The district includes six schools and includes [preschool](/wiki/Preschool "Preschool"), primary and secondary education.

The region is also home to the Silo School District, which includes three schools and serves approximately 680 students,[[49]](#cite_note-50) and to one of two schools in the Rock Creek School District.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

The city has a private [K-12](/wiki/K-12 "K-12") school, Victory Life Academy, which has an enrollment of about 250 students.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

### Roads and highways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Roads and highways")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Durant_hwy_69_75.jpg/250px-Durant_hwy_69_75.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durant_hwy_69_75.jpg)

Northbound Highway 69/75 in Durant

[US 69](/wiki/U.S._Route_69_in_Oklahoma "U.S. Route 69 in Oklahoma")/[US 75](/wiki/U.S._Route_75_in_Oklahoma "U.S. Route 75 in Oklahoma") – A north-south four-lane [divided highway](/wiki/Divided_highway "Divided highway") and freeway. US 69/75 enters Oklahoma as a freeway from the [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas%E2%80%93Fort_Worth_metroplex "Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex") area. It then downgrades to a divided four-lane highway just north of [Colbert](/wiki/Colbert,_Oklahoma "Colbert, Oklahoma"). The highway then enters Durant from the south. After its [at-grade intersection](/wiki/At-grade_intersection "At-grade intersection") with Choctaw Road, it again upgrades to a freeway, passing through western and northern Durant, and again downgrades to a four-lane divided highway at the Bryan-[Atoka County](/wiki/Atoka_County,_Oklahoma "Atoka County, Oklahoma") line.

[US 70](/wiki/U.S._Route_70_in_Oklahoma "U.S. Route 70 in Oklahoma") – An east–west route. Highway 70 enters Durant from the east as a two-lane highway as Mulberry Street, crossing a [Union Pacific](/wiki/Union_Pacific "Union Pacific") railroad via a bridge. It then heads southward toward downtown on First Avenue as a 3-lane, concurrent with SH 78 and Business Routes 69/75. Downtown it has an intersection with Main Street, then continues its route westward on West Main Street concurrent with Business Routes 69/75. Just west of downtown it turns into a three-lane street, and after its intersection with 9th Avenue it converts into a 5-lane street. At Washington Avenue it downgrades to a two-lane again while it travels through West End Heights, a [historic](/wiki/Historic_district "Historic district") and upscale neighborhood. At 21st Avenue it turns into a divided four-lane highway in the Retail District, intersecting with 69/75 as a [parclo interchange](/wiki/Parclo_interchange "Parclo interchange"). Highway 70 continues westward toward [Mead](/wiki/Mead,_Oklahoma "Mead, Oklahoma") and [Lake Texoma](/wiki/Lake_Texoma "Lake Texoma") as a 5-lane highway.

[SH 78](/wiki/Oklahoma_State_Highway_78 "Oklahoma State Highway 78") – A north–south route. SH 78 enters Durant from the south as Southeast 3rd Avenue and as a two-lane. It comes to an intersection with East Main Street and turns westward toward downtown continuing its route. At the intersection of Main and First Avenue, the highway turns northward onto First Avenue as a 3-lane street, concurrent with Highway 70 and Business Routes 69/75. It continues northward to University Boulevard and turns into a two-lane highway. Just before its intersection with 69/75 (via ramps), SH 78 turns into a divided four-lane highway for a short time and then turns into a two-lane highway again, exiting the city.

[Intercity bus service](/wiki/Intercity_bus_service "Intercity bus service") is provided by [Greyhound Lines](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines").[[53]](#cite_note-54)

In a 2006 study by the [Oklahoma Department of Transportation](/wiki/Oklahoma_Department_of_Transportation "Oklahoma Department of Transportation"), an average of about 19,100 vehicles pass Durant's Main Street on [U.S. 69](/wiki/U.S._Route_69 "U.S. Route 69")/[75](/wiki/U.S._Route_75 "U.S. Route 75") every day. Approximately 19,000 vehicles pass under 69/75 on Main Street daily.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

The major streets are First Avenue, Southeast 3rd Avenue, South 9th Avenue, Washington Avenue, Radio Road, University Place, 49th Avenue, Choctaw Road, Rodeo Road, West Main Street, East Main Street, University Boulevard, Chuckwa Street, Gail Farrell Drive, and Mockingbird Lane.

There are four exits in Durant from U.S. 69/75, at First Avenue, Washington Avenue, University/Chuckwa (the exit off northbound 69/75 is the only one complete), and Main Street. There are also [traffic lights](/wiki/Traffic_light "Traffic light") at the intersection of U.S. 69/75 and Choctaw Road south of Durant, where the Choctaw Casino Resort is located.

### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Airports")]

[Durant Regional Airport – Eaker Field](/wiki/Durant_Regional_Airport_%E2%80%93_Eaker_Field "Durant Regional Airport – Eaker Field"), the city's airport, and home to [Southeastern Oklahoma State University](/wiki/Southeastern_Oklahoma_State_University "Southeastern Oklahoma State University")'s Aviation Sciences Institute, was a [U.S. Navy](/wiki/U.S._Navy "U.S. Navy") auxiliary airfield during World War II. It is named after U.S. Army Air Force General [Ira C. Eaker](/wiki/Ira_C._Eaker "Ira C. Eaker"), early commander of the legendary [Eighth Air Force](/wiki/Eighth_Air_Force "Eighth Air Force") in wartime England, who graduated from the university (then known as Southeastern State Teacher's College) in 1917.

The closest [international airports](/wiki/International_airport "International airport") to Durant are [Dallas/Fort Worth International Airport](/wiki/Dallas/Fort_Worth_International_Airport "Dallas/Fort Worth International Airport") and [Will Rogers World Airport](/wiki/Will_Rogers_World_Airport "Will Rogers World Airport") in [Oklahoma City](/wiki/Oklahoma_City "Oklahoma City").

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Rail")]

Durant is a major railroad center. The giant [Union Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Railroad "Union Pacific Railroad") and the short-line [Kiamichi Railroad](/wiki/Kiamichi_Railroad "Kiamichi Railroad") intersect in downtown.

### Utilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Utilities")]

Durant is served by a city-owned water plant and sewage treatment center. Residents receive electricity from OG&E and Southeastern Electric Co-op.

### Healthcare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Healthcare")]

Durant is served by [AllianceHealth Durant](/wiki/AllianceHealth_Durant "AllianceHealth Durant"), which was formerly the Medical Center of Southeastern Oklahoma.[[55]](#cite_note-56) Built in 1987, the medical center replaced the Bryan Memorial Hospital.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Emergency Medical Services are provided by Bryan County EMS.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

## Popular culture references

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Popular culture references")]

* *[The Wendell Baker Story](/wiki/The_Wendell_Baker_Story "The Wendell Baker Story")* – A reference is made in the 2005 film to Neil King ([Owen Wilson](/wiki/Owen_Wilson "Owen Wilson"))'s mother living "nine miles due south of Durant, Oklahoma."[[59]](#cite_note-60)
* *[All About Steve](/wiki/All_About_Steve "All About Steve")* – In the 2009 film, [Sandra Bullock](/wiki/Sandra_Bullock "Sandra Bullock")'s character, Mary Horowitz, tells her friends "...if I had a car, I could drive through Durant, Oklahoma, where they have the world's largest peanut."
* *[On the Road with Austin & Santino](/wiki/On_the_Road_with_Austin_%26_Santino "On the Road with Austin & Santino")* – Durant was prominently featured in the 2010 episode "Wedding Knots", as the two designers created a wedding dress for a local woman. Local businesses visited include the [Choctaw Casino Resort](/wiki/Choctaw_Casino_Resort "Choctaw Casino Resort"), Bliss Boutique, and Doylene's Fabric Outlet.[[60]](#cite_note-61)
* *[Criminal Minds](/wiki/Criminal_Minds "Criminal Minds")* – In the 2011 episode "Proof," the team of investigators are led to a case in Durant involving two young women who were found murdered and sexually assaulted.[[61]](#cite_note-62) While set in Durant, the production never actually filmed any material there. Many locals were critical of the show and its unrealistic portrayal of the city.
* *[Bonnie & Clyde](/wiki/Bonnie_%26_Clyde_(2013_miniseries) "Bonnie & Clyde (2013 miniseries)")* – In this 2013 mini-series, Durant, Oklahoma is referenced in a newspaper article that appears on screen. It reads "Barrow Gang spotted in Durant, Oklahoma," with a sub-headline reading "Three Businesses Robbed At Gunpoint. Outlaws wanted in multiple states." True to the mini-series' inspiration, this was based upon true events.
* The Small Business Revolution - Durant saw an exciting end to 2018 and subsequent beginning to 2019 with a spot in competition to be the location for filming of Season 4 of The Small Business Revolution, a television show produced by [Deluxe Corporation](/wiki/Deluxe_Corporation "Deluxe Corporation") for distribution on [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu") and [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube"). The town applied to be considered in August 2018. The announcement was made in November that Durant was included as a finalist in a list of Top 20. The following month, Durant made the top 10. In January, the producers, along with host, Amanda Brinkman, visited the community for a welcome rally and to visit with small business owners who had applied to appear on the show, should Durant win the competition.[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64) In a live web announcement on February 12, Brinkman, along with co-host [Ty Pennington](/wiki/Ty_Pennington "Ty Pennington"), announced that there had been a slight change in the competition, with 6 finalists, rather than 5, proceeding to the final weeklong public vote. As a part of the campaign for votes, Durant garnered shout outs from [Reba McEntire](/wiki/Reba_McEntire "Reba McEntire"), [Blake Shelton](/wiki/Blake_Shelton "Blake Shelton"), and [Barry Switzer](/wiki/Barry_Switzer "Barry Switzer"), among others. During the weeklong public vote, the show made three announcements as to where the votes stood, with Durant in first place as of the final update. At the conclusion of voting, with a week to go before the announcement of the winning town, Durant went to work planning for the #MyDurant party, where Brinkman would appear if Durant should win. Ultimately, [Searcy, Arkansas](/wiki/Searcy,_Arkansas "Searcy, Arkansas") was announced as the winner. The competition gave the city a boost in morale and saw the city work together toward the goal of winning the show. Despite it all, Durant was featured in several blogs published by the production.[[64]](#cite_note-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66)

## Other distinctions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durant,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Other distinctions")]

Durant has had the honor to be recognized on a national scale on various occasions, regarding famous visitations or other attention. An example of this is as follows:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/President_Theodore_Roosevelt_Speaking_from_a_Railroad_Observation_Car_%2815013237379%29.jpg/250px-President_Theodore_Roosevelt_Speaking_from_a_Railroad_Observation_Car_%2815013237379%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Theodore_Roosevelt_Speaking_from_a_Railroad_Observation_Car_(15013237379).jpg)

President Theodore Roosevelt addressed the community from the back of his train car on April 5, 1905.

* On April 5, 1905, President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") arrived in Durant on a train, and gave a speech, using the rear of the train as his platform.[[66]](#cite_note-67) He later revisited the community in 1910, staying in the downtown Bryan Hotel, while on a hunting trip in the area.[[67]](#cite_note-68)
* First Lady [Eleanor Roosevelt](/wiki/Eleanor_Roosevelt "Eleanor Roosevelt") visited the community, speaking on March 20, 1937, for Senior Day at [Southeastern Teachers College](/wiki/Southeastern_Oklahoma_State_University "Southeastern Oklahoma State University"), as it was known at the time. She spoke at the invitation of the president of the college, [Kate Galt Zaneis](/wiki/Kate_Galt_Zaneis "Kate Galt Zaneis"). Mrs. Roosevelt noted in her diary, "I spoke to a packed auditorium of young people, who proved to be a remarkably good audience." As Mrs. Roosevelt stepped up to speak, the orchestra played "Let Me Call You Sweetheart." Mrs. Zaneis said the song was chosen because Mrs. Roosevelt was loved so much.[[68]](#cite_note-69) Mrs. Roosevelt received around 2300 people that afternoon. She notes "I rarely do this, but it is also rare for a woman to be a president of a college where there are both men and women students." She also wrote that upon boarding the train to leave town, some of the students waiting at the depot assisted her in loading fifty-four boxes of flowers as gifts to her onto the train.[[69]](#cite_note-70)
* In 1953, actor [José Ferrer](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Ferrer "José Ferrer") and actress/singer [Rosemary Clooney](/wiki/Rosemary_Clooney "Rosemary Clooney") were married in Durant while Jose was performing in the musical *[Kiss Me, Kate](/wiki/Kiss_Me,_Kate "Kiss Me, Kate")* in [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas").[[70]](#cite_note-71)
* According to papers from the FBI released during the drop of Kennedy documents released in 2017, [Jack Ruby](/wiki/Jack_Ruby "Jack Ruby") went to Durant to look at purchasing a motel just north of town in 1960. He was accompanied by Roy Kimes and Joe Russell Wyatt to area, dropping off Wyatt in [Colbert](/wiki/Colbert,_Oklahoma "Colbert, Oklahoma") and subsequently picking him up on the way back to [Farmersville](/wiki/Farmersville,_Texas "Farmersville, Texas").[[71]](#cite_note-72)
* On June 2, 1965, less than two years after her [former husband](/wiki/Lee_Harvey_Oswald "Lee Harvey Oswald") was believed to have shot President [John F Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") (and was subsequently shot himself), [Marina Oswald](/wiki/Marina_Oswald_Porter "Marina Oswald Porter") and fiancé Kenneth Porter, came to Durant to get married due to the blood testing laws of the time. They arrived early in the day to take the blood tests, but were told to return at 2pm for the results. They ate lunch and drove out to [Lake Texoma](/wiki/Lake_Texoma "Lake Texoma") to pass time before 2 pm. By the time 2pm came around, the media had caught up to them and they fled to [Sherman, Texas](/wiki/Sherman,_Texas "Sherman, Texas") to avoid the attention. They were eventually married that evening in [Fate, Texas](/wiki/Fate,_Texas "Fate, Texas").[[72]](#cite_note-73)[[73]](#cite_note-74)
* On July 15, 2015, President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") visited the city to address the nation from Durant High School on his "Promised Zone" initiative.[[74]](#cite_note-75) In 2014, the President designated five areas in the United States as Promised Zones, including the [Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma](/wiki/Choctaw_Nation_of_Oklahoma "Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma"). The other areas designated were Los Angeles, Philadelphia, San Antonio, and Southeastern Kentucky. The initiative was "designed to create jobs and increase economic security by partnering local communities with business." While speaking, he also introduced his ConnectHome initiative, which is designed to open up greater access to the internet for low income households.[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77)
* In August 2016, Durant found itself at the unlikely center of controversy after an online petition was created by a citizen of [Edmond, Oklahoma](/wiki/Edmond,_Oklahoma "Edmond, Oklahoma"), Ryan Nazari, suggesting that Durant should be forced to change its name to Westbrook in honor of [Russell Westbrook](/wiki/Russell_Westbrook "Russell Westbrook"), after [Kevin Durant](/wiki/Kevin_Durant "Kevin Durant")'s unpopular departure from the [Oklahoma City Thunder](/wiki/Oklahoma_City_Thunder "Oklahoma City Thunder"). "Ladies and gentleman, the great state of Oklahoma has been betrayed...It is because of this heinous action that I believe the State of Oklahoma has a responsibility to change the name of the City of Durant to Westbrook, the man who is loyal, whom we believe in, and who will lead our team to glory. Yes, it is understood that the city Durant was not named after the evil Kevin Durant, but it is just another hideous reminder of what happened," Nazari's petition read. Neither the creator of the petition, nor Kevin Durant had ever visited the community. The news surrounding the petition led to an article by [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN")[[77]](#cite_note-78) and later by [The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times").[[78]](#cite_note-79)
* In December 2016, Durant gained national attention again. *[People](/wiki/People_(magazine) "People (magazine)")* published a light-hearted article regarding the Durant Fire Department's annual Christmas Card. The department had experienced the birth of six babies during the year and decided to feature them in their annual Christmas card. "If you ask many of the fire fighters in Durant, OK what defines them; they will say brotherhood and family. That is why when this small paid department of 33 looked back on the year and saw that 6 of their brothers had newborn babies, it was time to show the public what was at the core of their department."[[79]](#cite_note-80)
